steps.text: Measure the gas temperature at the entrance to the adsorbent module.
Use a water-jacketed glass container to hold up to 40 grams of the solid adsorbent.
Orient the adsorbent module vertically to facilitate condensate drainage.
Ensure connecting fittings form leak-free, vacuum-tight seals.
Include a coarse glass frit in the adsorbent module to retain the adsorbent.
Connect five impingers in series with leak-free ground glass fittings.
Use a short-stem design or equivalent for the first impinger.
Modify the second, fourth, and fifth impingers with a specific tip design.
Ensure the second and third impingers contain known quantities of water.
Ensure the fifth impinger contains a known weight of silica gel or equivalent desiccant.
Cap the sections of the sampling train exposed to the sample gas with leak-free ground glass fittings.
Use PTFE bottles.
Use inert bristle brushes with precleaned stainless steel or PTFE handles.
Use a sealed filter holder, wide-mouth amber glass jar with PTFE-lined cap, or glass petri dish sealed with PTFE tape for filter storage.
Use a weighing device capable of measurements to an accuracy of 0.5 g.
Use heavy duty aluminum foil cleaned by rinsing three times with hexane or toluene for aluminum foil tasks.
Use an air-tight container to store silica gel.

constraints.text: Do not use aluminum foil to wrap or contact filter samples due to the possibility of reaction between the sample and the aluminum.

steps.text: Conduct a QC check on the cleaned adsorbent lot or batch following the extraction and analyses procedures in Section 11 of this method, including adding applicable labeled standards.
Store adsorbent in a solvent-rinsed nonporous clean container and secure lid.
Clean the glass wool to meet the specifications in Section 13.1 of this method.
Use deionized or distilled water meeting requirements in Section 13.1 of this method and store in its original container or in a clean glass container with a PTFE-lined screw cap.

Use indicating type silica gel for sampling, 6–16 mesh. If previously used, dry at 175 °C (347 °F) for two hours. Use new silica gel as received. As an alternative, use other types of desiccants (equivalent or better), subject to the approval of the Administrator.
Use pesticide grade or better Methylene Chloride.
Use pesticide grade or better Acetone.
Use pesticide grade or better Toluene.
Use American Chemical Society (ACS) grade Potassium Hydroxide, 2% (weight/volume) in water.
Use granulated or powdered Sodium Sulfate, reagent grade. Evaluate for cleanliness prior to use with an LMB.
Use reagent grade Sulfuric Acid.
Weigh 40 g of Sodium Hydroxide into a 1-liter volumetric flask. Dilute to 1 liter with water.
Use pesticide grade or better Hexane.
Use pesticide grade or better Methanol.
Use pesticide grade or better Toluene.
Use High-Boiling Alkanes as Keeper Solvents (e.g., tetradecane, nonane, decane), pesticide grade.
Use liquid column chromatography packing materials to prepare sample extracts by fractionation and removal of interferences.
Evaluate alternative carbon-based sorbents for sample cleanup prior to their use.
Prepare an 18% weight/weight mixture of Carbopack® C and Celite® 545 and activate at 130 °C for a minimum of 6 hours.
Allow the mixture to cool and store in a desiccator.
Prepare spiking standards quantitatively at a convenient concentration or use commercial standards if available.
Adjust the sample fortification concentrations to accommodate the concentration of target compounds.
Prepare stock standard solutions in nonane for pre-sampling adsorbent standard.
Prepare stock standard solutions in nonane for pre-extraction filter recovery standard.
Prepare stock standard solutions in nonane for pre-extraction standard.
Prepare stock standard solutions in nonane for pre-analysis standard.
Weigh several 200 to 300 g portions of silica gel in an air-tight container to the nearest 0.5 g.
Record the total weight of the silica gel plus container, on the outside of each container.
As an alternative, directly weigh the silica gel in its impinger or sampling holder just prior to sampling.
Check each filter against light for irregularities and flaws or pinhole leaks.
Pack the filters flat in a clean glass container.
Do not mark filters with ink or any other contaminating substance.
Sample for a minimum of 2 minutes at each traverse point.
Use the same sampling time at each traverse point for continuously operating processes.
Determine the minimum operating cycle duration for batch processes, dividing the sampling time evenly between the required numbers of traverse points.
Sample all traverse points at least once during each test run.
Keep all train openings where contamination can enter sealed until just prior to assembly or until sampling is about to begin.
Wrap the adsorbent module with aluminum foil or other suitable material capable of shielding the module from light.
Weigh each impinger and the adsorbent module, including the fitting caps, to the nearest 0.5 g using the field balance and record the weight for moisture determination.
Remove the aluminum foil from the adsorbent module before weighing.
Keep the module out of direct sunlight and rewrap the module with foil immediately after recording the module weight.

preconditions.text: Ensure that the appropriate silica gel portions are ready for weighing before starting step 1.
Ensure that filters are clean and free of ink or any contaminating substance before starting step 4.
Ensure that the sampling apparatus is prepared and all safety measures are in place before starting step 11.

steps.text: Tune the HRMS instrument to a resolving power of at least 10,000 at 10% percent of the peak height or 25,000 at 50% percent of the peak height.
Assess the resolution at three exact m/z’s representing the low-, mid-, and high-m/z range of the masses used to measure the target compound class.
Calibrate the HRGC/HRMS system using a minimum of five concentrations over a range that brackets expected field sample concentrations and the concentration of isotopically labeled.
Determine the initial relative response factors for the target compounds and isotopically labeled standards using the initial calibration.
Monitor the QC check ions specified in these tables to verify instrument stability during the analysis.
Conduct additional cleanup of the sample extract and reanalysis if failure to maintain the lock-mass during analysis occurs.
Prepare the CCV standard at the same time as the batch of field samples using the same labeled standards.
Inject a CCV standard for the target compound class at least once every 12 hours during an analysis sequence.
Calculate the RRF for each compound and compare each RRF to the corresponding mean RRF obtained during the initial calibration.
Ensure the RRF for each native compound measured in a CCV does not deviate from the initial calibration RRF by more than the limits shown in Table 23–14.
Repeat the initial calibration when there is a failure to meet the requirements for acceptable CCV standard analysis.
Use the results from a CCV to verify and document the resolution required in Section 13.2, 13.3, or 13.4 of this method for the target compound classes analyzed with this method.
Perform the resolution check in Section 10.2.3.5 of this method to document the required resolution on the confirmation column if you use a confirmation column.
Concentrate the sample to a volume of no less than 5 mL.
Concentrate samples containing toluene using a heating mantle and three-ball Snyder column or a rotary evaporator.
Rinse sample Container No. 2 three times with small portions of toluene and add these to the concentrated solution and concentrate further to no less than 5 mL.
Rinse the concentrated material from Container No. 2 into the glass extraction thimble containing the filter and the XAD–2 resin.
Transfer the solvent contained in the glass solvent rinse catch beaker to the extraction apparatus solvent reservoir.
Rinse the beaker into the Soxhlet extraction apparatus solvent reservoir three times with small portions of toluene.
Transfer the contents of Container No. 3 to a separatory funnel.
Adjust to pH 2 with 6 N sulfuric acid, if necessary.
Rinse the sample container with three successive 10-mL aliquots of toluene and add these rinses to the separatory funnel.
Extract the sample by vigorously shaking the separatory funnel for 5 minutes.
After complete separation of the phases, remove the solvent and filter it through a bed of precleaned, dry sodium sulfate into the Soxhlet extraction apparatus solvent reservoir.
Repeat the extraction step two additional times.

Adjust the pH to 11 with 6 N sodium hydroxide, re-extract the impinger water and rinses, and filter it through a bed of precleaned, dry sodium sulfate into the Soxhlet extraction apparatus solvent reservoir.
Rinse the sodium sulfate into the extraction apparatus solvent reservoir with fresh solvent and discard the sodium sulfate.
Add the appropriate pre-extraction standard for the target compound classes to the extraction thimble containing the combined filter and adsorbent sample fractions.
Cover the contents of the extraction thimble with the cleaned glass wool plug to prevent the XAD–2 resin from splashing into the solvent reservoir of the extractor.
Place the extraction thimble into the Soxhlet extraction apparatus.
Pour additional toluene to fill the solvent reservoir to approximately two-thirds capacity.
Add PTFE boiling chips and assemble the apparatus.
Adjust the heat source to cause the extractor to cycle approximately three times per hour.
Extract the sample for sufficient time to meet the pre-extraction standard recovery performance criteria.
The solvent should cycle completely through the system a minimum of 48 times.
Allow the Soxhlet apparatus to cool.
You may perform an initial concentration of the sample extract using the techniques.
Concentrate initial extracts in toluene using a heating mantle and three-ball Snyder column or a rotary evaporator.
Concentrate the field train proof blank and LMB samples in the same manner as samples.
Allow the sample extract to cool.
Use a minimum of one half of the sample extract for PCDD/PCDF analysis.
Archive the remaining sample extract or further split the sample extract for PCB and/or PAH analysis and archive.
Further concentrate the sample extract for cleanup and analysis using concentration techniques.
Process a separate aliquot/split of the sample extract for each of the compound classes analyzed by this method.
Evaluate the cleanup and fractionation procedures used to confirm acceptable recovery of isotopically labeled standards.
Use acid, neutral, and basic silica gel and cleanup procedures to remove nonpolar and polar interferences from samples destined for PCB and PCDD/PCDF analysis.
Use Carbopack®/Celite® (or other equivalent carbon-based column material) to remove other nonpolar interferences.
Use a Florisil® column for PAH and PCDPE fractionation and cleanup.

preconditions.text: If using amount other than half the sample extract, adjust the spiking amount of the labeled standards accordingly.
Confirm that the LMB is free from contamination as defined in Section 13.1.

steps.text: Remove both the salt solution and any residual extractable chemicals used in the polymerization process before use.
Prepare the resin by a series of water and organic extractions, followed by careful drying.
Place resin in a suitable container, soak for approximately 5 min with Type II water, remove fine floating resin particles and discard the water.
Fill with Type II water a second time, let stand overnight, remove fine floating resin particles, and discard the water.
Extract with water for 8 hr.
Extract for 22 hr with methyl alcohol.
Extract for 22 hr with methylene chloride.
Extract for 22 hr with toluene.
Extract for 22 hr with methylene chloride.
Store the resin in a sealed glass container filled with toluene prior to the final toluene extraction.
Repeat the final methylene chloride extractions to meet the cleanliness requirements in Section 13.1 of Method 23.
Construct any size extractor as needed for the sampling programs.
Hold the resin in a glass or stainless-steel cylinder between a pair of coarse and fine screens.
Circulate clean solvent through the resin for extraction.
Maintain a flow rate upward through the resin to allow maximum solvent contact and prevent channeling.
Perform the water extraction at the rate of about 20 to 40 mL/min.
Use a small pump for pumping the water through the canister.
Ensure all materials of construction are glass, PTFE, or stainless steel.
Ensure pumps, if used, should not contain extractable materials.
